coredata.dc:title: Endogenous NO-releasing Carbon Nanodots for Tumor-specific Gas Therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xue
Liu, Yulin
Thakor, Avnesh S.
Kevadiya, Bhavesh D.
Cheng, Jiamin
Chen, Manling
Li, Yang
Xu, Qian
Wu, Qiuhua
Wu, Yang
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon nanodots based on L-arginine (L-Arg) were developed for enhanced nitric oxide (NO) gas therapy for cancer. The L-Arg-based carbon nanodots (Arg-dots) produced high levels of NO in the tumor environment rich in endogenous H2O2. In vitro cell experiments revealed that the Arg-dots could kill tumor cells (including human breast cancer cell line MCF-7, female gastric cancer cell line BGC-823, male lung cancer cell line A549, and female leukemic cell line K562) but did not affect the activity of normal cells (human normal lung epithelial cell line BEAS-2B). The Arg-dots produced twice the amount of NO for an equivalent amount of L-Arg. Theoretical calculations showed that the carbonization structure of the Arg-dots promoted significantly more electrons toward the guanidinium groups of L-Arg and boosted the adsorption of H2O2 molecules. In vitro and in vivo investigations confirmed that the Arg-dots reduced the multidrug resistance (MDR) effect of the tumor cells (MCF-7/ADR cells) and produced a combined antitumor efficacy with traditional chemotherapeutic drugs (adriamycin [ADR]). The fluorescence property (quantum yield, 6.88%) allows the Arg-dots to be used as a suitable fluorescent probe for fluorescence imaging of tumor cells. The ultra-small size of the Arg-dots (diameter: ca. 2.5 nm) enables them not only to penetrate deep tumors and provide enhanced antitumor activity but also to be removed through kidney filtration and have a renal clearance property.
Statement of significance Nitric oxide (NO), which serves as a biological messenger, can be used in gas therapy for cancer. The development of a safe and efficient NO cancer therapy is, however, challenging because of the low NO release amount and poor tumor specificity of most NO donors. Many efforts have been made to overcome these drawbacks, but solving both these limitations through a single approach has been seldom achieved. In the present work, carbon nanodots (Arg-dots) from L-arginine were used for gas therapy of cancer. The Arg-dots produced NO in the H2O2-rich tumor environment. Theoretical calculations were consistent with the mechanism of enhanced NO release amount. The Arg-dots also reduced the multidrug resistance effect in cancer chemotherapy. In vivo and in vitro toxicity assessments confirmed that the Arg-dots have excellent biosafety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: nanomedicine
tumor microenvironment
density functional theory
Sakaguchi reaction
guanidinium groups
fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In Vivo Evaluation of the Curative Effect

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51873085 ) and LiaoNing Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2007056).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the Arg-dots and their NO-releasing process in tumor cells.

(A) TEM image of the Arg-dots (Inset: size distribution and HRTEM image of the Arg-dots); (B) FT-IR spectrum of the Arg-dots; (C) XPS high-resolution N1s spectra of the Arg-dots; (D) UV–Vis spectrum of the colored product formed from the Arg-dots after the Sakaguchi reaction.

(A) PL spectra (excitation wavelength: 310–500 nm) of the Arg-dots (Inset: photograph of the Arg-dot aqueous solution, excitation wavelength: 370 nm); inverted fluorescent microscopy images of A549 cells after successive incubation with (B) the Arg-dots (excitation wavelength: 365 nm) and (C) DAF-FM DA (3-amino, 4-aminomethyl-2ʹ,7ʹ-difluorescein) (excitation wavelength: 470 nm); (D) The merged fluorescent image of the Arg-dots and DAF-FM DA (Scale bars = 100 μm).

(A) The NO release profiles of the Arg-dots incubated with various concentrations of H2O2 in PBS; (B) The NO release profiles of L-Arg and Arg-dots incubated with 12.5 mM of H2O2 in PBS. Data in (A) and (B) are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3); (C) The simulated model of the Arg-dots (carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen atoms are shown in gray, blue, red, and white, respectively; bond lengths are expressed in angstrom); (D) The charge density difference for the Arg-dots (charge accumulation is shown as the yellow region, and charge depletion is shown as the blue region; the C...N bond is indicated by a dotted red circle); (E) The total electronic density of state (TDOS, black line) and partial density of state (PDOS, red line for N-doped graphite and blue line for L-Arg) for the Arg-dots; (F) The density of state (DOS) for the nitrogen atom of the guanidinium groups of L-Arg (black line) and the Arg-dots (red line) (the green dotted line indicates the Fermi level).

(A) In vitro toxicity assessment of the Arg-dots against MCF-7, BGC-823, A549, and K562 cancer cells and BEAS-2B normal cells; (B) In vitro toxicity assessment of the Arg-dots against MCF-7/ADR cells; (C) Images present the growth inhibition efficacy of MCF-7/ADR tumors in mice after treatment with various samples; (D) Calculated weights of the corresponding explanted tumor tissues; (E) Calculated volumes of the corresponding explanted tumor tissues; (F) The body weight of the mice in each group. Data in (A) and (B) are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3), and data in (D), (E), and (F) are shown as mean ± SD (n = 5). *** p<0.001; **** p<0.0001.

(A) Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining images of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney from mice administered with PBS (control) and the Arg-dots, scale bar = 100 μm. The body weight of mice (B) in high-dose acute toxicity experiments and (C) in low-dose chronic toxicity experiments. Data in (B) and (C) are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Endogenous NO-releasing Carbon Nanodots for Tumor-specific Gas Therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Cell Biology, Key Laboratory of Cell Biology of Ministry of Public Health, Key Laboratory of Medical Cell Biology of Ministry of Education, China Medical University, 110122, Shenyang, Liaoning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots based on L-arginine (L-Arg) were developed for enhanced nitric oxide (NO) gas therapy for cancer. The L-Arg-based carbon nanodots (Arg-dots) produced high levels of NO in the tumor environment rich in endogenous H2O2. In vitro cell experiments revealed that the Arg-dots could kill tumor cells (including human breast cancer cell line MCF-7, female gastric cancer cell line BGC-823, male lung cancer cell line A549, and female leukemic cell line K562) but did not affect the activity of normal cells (human normal lung epithelial cell line BEAS-2B). The Arg-dots produced twice the amount of NO for an equivalent amount of L-Arg. Theoretical calculations showed that the carbonization structure of the Arg-dots promoted significantly more electrons toward the guanidinium groups of L-Arg and boosted the adsorption of H2O2 molecules. In vitro and in vivo investigations confirmed that the Arg-dots reduced the multidrug resistance (MDR) effect of the tumor cells (MCF-7/ADR cells) and produced a combined antitumor efficacy with traditional chemotherapeutic drugs (adriamycin [ADR]). The fluorescence property (quantum yield, 6.88%) allows the Arg-dots to be used as a suitable fluorescent probe for fluorescence imaging of tumor cells. The ultra-small size of the Arg-dots (diameter: ca. 2.5 nm) enables them not only to penetrate deep tumors and provide enhanced antitumor activity but also to be removed through kidney filtration and have a renal clearance property.

Nitric oxide (NO), which serves as a biological messenger, can be used in gas therapy for cancer. The development of a safe and efficient NO cancer therapy is, however, challenging because of the low NO release amount and poor tumor specificity of most NO donors. Many efforts have been made to overcome these drawbacks, but solving both these limitations through a single approach has been seldom achieved. In the present work, carbon nanodots (Arg-dots) from L-arginine were used for gas therapy of cancer. The Arg-dots produced NO in the H2O2-rich tumor environment. Theoretical calculations were consistent with the mechanism of enhanced NO release amount. The Arg-dots also reduced the multidrug resistance effect in cancer chemotherapy. In vivo and in vitro toxicity assessments confirmed that the Arg-dots have excellent biosafety.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO) is a ubiquitous gaseous signaling molecule that exists in almost all types of organisms, ranging from bacteria to plants and animal cells [1]. NO is produced from NO donors (such as L-arginine) through catalysis by nitric oxide synthase (NOS) [2,3] and plays vital roles in various physiological and pathological processes, including regulating cardiovascular homeostasis, promoting platelet aggregation and adhesion, triggering an immune response to infection, mediating neurotransmission, and exerting an antibacterial effect [4,5].

In recent years, the role of NO in cancer therapies has gained increasing attention of the scientific community. NO acts like a double-edged sword in cancer biology: lower concentrations (i.e., picomolar concentration) of NO can promote tumor growth, whereas higher levels (i.e., micromolar concentration) of NO can induce tumor apoptosis [6,7]. Therefore, there are two approaches to use NO in cancer treatment: anti- and pro-NO cancer therapies [8]. Anti-NO cancer therapies aim to decrease NO concentrations at the tumor site to initiate apoptosis and/or necrosis of cancer cells by using NOS inhibitors [9]. In contrast, pro-NO cancer therapies require NO donors to increase NO concentrations at the tumor site. High levels of NO can induce the nitrosation of mitochondria and DNA [10]. Anti-NO cancer therapies need long-term and systemic administration. Side effects such as hypertension and tumor recurrence may occur after termination of treatment [11]. Compared to anti-NO therapies, pro-NO cancer therapies are easier to manage. Because NO is a free radical, the direct delivery of NO is not reasonably practicable. NO donors are generally used in pro-NO cancer therapies. NO donors can be targeted to tumor sites by using targeting agents [12]. NO release can also be controlled on-demand through exogenous and endogenous stimuli of tumor environments [13]. Moreover, pro-NO cancer therapies can synergize and enhance the efficacy of other treatment strategies for tumors, such as radiotherapy or photodynamic therapy [14]. One of the significant obstacles in cancer chemotherapy is the multidrug resistance (MDR) effect [15]. NO has been confirmed to reverse the MDR effect by inhibiting the overexpression of P-glycoprotein transporters on the plasma membrane of tumor cells [16]. All these advantages together make it necessary to develop NO donors with high NO release property to enhance pro-NO cancer therapy.

The precise control over the delivery of NO is critical for the curative effect of pro-NO cancer therapies. One of the promising strategies to realize the accurate delivery of NO is to prepare NO donors in a nanoparticle form (NO nanodonors). NO nanodonors can improve the biological activities of NO by increasing solubility, stability, and targetability [17,18]. Many classes of NO nanodonors have been developed for rapidity and precise spatiotemporal control of NO delivery, including exogenous and endogenous NO nanodonors [19]. Exogenous NO donors can generate NO through external stimuli such as heat, light, and ultrasound [20,21]. Endogenous NO donors use the tumor-intrinsic microenvironment to release NO, such as acidic pH, glucose, glutathione, and H2O2 [22–24]. Compared to exogenous NO nanodonors, endogenous NO nanodonors are most suitable and convenient for achieving the goal of precise delivery of NO. Firstly, endogenous NO nanodonors can selectively release NO in tumors instead of normal tissues and achieve tumor-specific therapy. Secondly, possible side effects caused by external stimuli are also avoided, such as skin allergies or inflammation induced by light [25]. Thirdly, endogenous NO nanodonors can function as soon as they arrive at the tumor sites and thus achieve the precise spatiotemporal control of NO delivery. In short, endogenous NO nanodonors can enhance targeted pro-NO cancer treatment efficacy and reduce side effects.

Arginine (L-Arg), a natural endogenous NO donor, can release NO through catalysis by NOS [9,26]. Recent developments in research on L-Arg demonstrated that L-Arg can also generate NO in the H2O2-rich tumor microenvironment [27]. Some endogenous NO nanodonors have been prepared based on biocompatible L-Arg (Arg-nanodonors) [28]. However, a relatively low level of H2O2 concentration in the tumor microenvironment limits Arg-nanodonors to generate adequate NO for pro-NO cancer therapy. To address this issue, Chen's group introduced H2O2 donors to increase the H2O2 concentration in tumor sites [29]. This strategy can accelerate the oxidization of L-Arg into NO, but make the nanomedicine complex for clinical application. It remains challenging to develop a simple Arg-nanodonor with high activity for pro-NO cancer therapies.

In the present study, we prepared an Arg-nanodonor through a simple one-pot synthesis without any post-modification [30]. The Arg-nanodonor with characteristics of fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) was synthesized using L-Arg as a reagent and labeled as Arg-dots. The Arg-dots inherited the NO donor group (guanidinium group) from L-Arg. After being endocytosed by cancer cells, the Arg-dots can generate a large amount of intratumoral NO to kill cancer cells without any additional external triggers (Scheme 1 ). In addition to the antitumor effect, the Arg-dots could also reduce the MDR effect of tumors and achieve an enhanced therapeutic effect in combination with traditional chemotherapeutic drugs (adriamycin, ADR). Furthermore, the Arg-dots could act as a fluorescent emitter and enable tumor imaging.

Na2HPO4•12H2O, L-arginine, NaNO2, and sulfanilamide (SULF) were purchased from Innochem Technology (Beijing, China). Ethylenediamine was ordered from YongDa Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). H2O2 (30%) was supplied by the Damao Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). N-(1-naphthyl)-ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NEED) was obtained from Aladdin Industry Corporation (Shanghai, China). The reagents in cell experiments were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Company Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethanol absolute and neutral balsam were purchased from GH Tech (Shantou, China). Hematoxylin was supplied by Leagene (Beijing, China). Eosin and ADR were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Inc. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from Boster (Wuhan, China). Hydrochloric acid (36%∼38%) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Xylene was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Li & Li Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Penicillin-Streptomycin (P/S) Solution was purchased from Li Rui Biological Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All cell culture plates were obtained from NEST Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuxi, China). All cell culture media were purchased from Corning Co., Ltd. (Corning, USA). 4-Amino-5-methylamino-2’,7’-difluorofluorescein diacetate (DAF-FM DA) and methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Paraformaldehyde was obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Female BALB/c mice were supplied by the Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) and were used in the in vivo curative effect assessment experiments. Female C57/BL mice were supplied by the Animal Institute of Southern Medical University (Guangzhou, China) and were used in the in vivo toxicity assessment experiments. Paraformaldehyde, Triton X-100, and 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine dihydrochloride (DAPI) were obtained from Solarbio Life Science Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd. (Waltham, USA). Rabbit P-glycoprotein primary antibody was obtained from Wanleibio Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Goat polyclonal anti-rabbit IgG H&L secondary antibody conjugated with Alexa Fluor 594 was obtained from Abcam Plc Co., Ltd. (Cambridge, UK). All the other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All the solvents were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water was used for solution preparations if no special instructions were provided.

The Arg-dots were prepared by a microwave method. Briefly, 5.74 mmol of L-arginine and 2.88 mmol of ethylenediamine were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL). The mixture was added to an open wide-necked conical flask (100 mL) without a stopper and then heated in a domestic microwave oven (Midea Microwave 20 L, 700 W, Digital, M1-L202B, Foshan, China) for 4 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the white and foamy solid Arg-dots were obtained. The crude Arg-dots were then dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da, Spectrum Labs, USA) for 48 h to remove the excess precursors and small molecules. The pure Arg-dots were finally stored as freeze-dried powder.

UV–Vis data were collected on a Purkinje General TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Purkinje General, Beijing, China). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were recorded using an RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Both the excitation and emission slits were set as 5 and 5 nm, respectively. Microwave reactions were conducted using a domestic microwave oven at the power of 750 W. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were collected by an FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using a K-alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, USA). The morphology and microstructure of the Arg-dots were examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The samples for HRTEM were prepared by dropping an aqueous solution onto a 200-mesh copper grid coated with a lacy carbon film. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed on a laser light scattering spectrometer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK) equipped with a laser source of 560 nm. The cell viabilities were detected using a microplate reader (Tecan Infinite M1000 PRO, Hombrechtikon, Switzerland). Inverted fluorescent microscopy images were collected by a Nikon Ts2-FL inverted microscope and an Axio Scope A1 microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Flow cytometry analysis was conducted using a flow cytometer (FACSCalibur, Becton Dickinson, San Jose, USA). Fluorescent analysis of P-glycoprotein in MCF-7/ADR cells was performed using a laser scan confocal microscope (FV3000, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) with the same exposure settings for each comparison group.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (Ф) of the Arg-dots was calculated with a comparative method. Quinine sulfate with a standard fluorescence QY (54%), the maximum emission wavelength (450 nm) of which was close to that of the Arg-dots (440 nm), was chosen as the reference to determine the fluorescence QY of the Arg-dots. First, the standard curve of quinine sulfate was plotted. A series of quinine sulfate solutions in 0.1 M H2SO4 were prepared with different absorbance values. To minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbance values were not allowed to exceed 0.1, according to the size of the fluorescence cuvette we used (10 mm). The fluorescence spectra of these quinine sulfate solutions were recorded under the optimum excitation wavelength (generally the wavelength of the maximum absorbance), and the integral area values of the fluorescence spectra were calculated. A graph was plotted using the integral area values against the maximum absorbance values, and a trend line was added for the curve with an intercept at zero to obtain the gradient as GR . Using a similar method, GT because of the pending Arg-dots can be achieved. The fluorescence QY of the Arg-dots was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Φ
T
=
Φ
R
(
G T
G R
)
(
η T 2
η R 2
)
T represents test samples;
R represents standard samples;
Φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield of test samples;
G represents the gradient from the plot of the integral area of the fluorescence spectrum under the optimum excitation wavelength vs the maximum absorbance;
η represents the refractive index of the solvent;
where ФR is the known fluorescence QY of quinine sulfate (54%). The fluorescence QY of the Arg-dots was determined as 6.88%.

The Arg-dots (200 mg) were dissolved in PBS (5 mL) and then transferred into a dialysis bag. The sealed dialysis bag was placed into a centrifuge tube (30 mL), and 15 mL PBS was added to the centrifuge tube as the dialysate. H2O2 at different concentrations were added to the dialysate, and the mixture was stirred at 37°C. The production of NO was detected as nitrite (NO2 −) through a typical Griess assay. In Griess assay, a chromophore was achieved by coupling acidic nitrite diazotizing sulfanilamide and bicyclic amines such as NEED. In this study, 100 μL dialysate was taken periodically from the centrifuge tube as the pending solution. Next, 50 μL Griess Reagent I (1% sulfanilamide) and 50 μL Griess Reagent II (0.1% NEED) were added to the pending solution. After 20 min of incubation, the absorbance of the mixed solution was measured at 540 nm for the quantitative analysis of NO by using a standard curve. The NO release amount in this study was expressed as the cumulative release amount. A similar approach was used for determining NO release from Arg with an equal amount to Arg-dots (10 mg/mL).

CDs are zero-dimensional carbon material with a diameter less than 10 nm [31]. There is almost no overlap between the conduction band and the valence band of CDs; hence, electron transfer on CDs is hardly affected [32]. Therefore, the CDs were simulated by using nitrogen-doped monolayer graphene. The pristine graphene surface consisting of 72 carbon atoms was constructed with 6 carbon atom rings along the x-axis and the y-axis. The nitrogen-doped graphite structure was created by replacing 2 carbon atoms from the surface of pristine graphene by 2 nitrogen atoms (Figure S1B). The surface area of the nitrogen-doped graphite structure was 14.76 Å × 14.76 Å. The C-N bond length in the carbon atom ring was 1.42 Å. A periodic boundary condition was used to model this surface, and a vacuum slab with a thickness of around 10 Å was investigated on the nitrogen-doped graphite structure. The Arg-dots were simulated by using an L-Arg-modified nitrogen-doped graphite structure (Figure S1C).

All the density functional theory (DFT) computations were performed using Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) [33]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the exchange-correlation functional of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) was used [34], with a semi-empirical van der Waals (vdW) correction to account for the dispersion interactions [35]. The electron-ion interactions were described with the projector augmented wave (PAW) method [36,37], and the energy cutoff for the plane-wave expansion was set at 450 eV [38]. The convergence tolerance of the maximum energy and the maximum force on each atom during structure relaxation was less than 10−5 eV and 0.02 eV•Å−1, respectively. Monkhorst–Pack meshes of 3 × 3 × 1 was used for geometry optimization and the calculation of density of states [39].

The human female breast cancer cell line (MCF-7), human female breast cancer ADR-resistant cell line (MCF-7/ADR), human male lung cancer cell line (A549), human female gastric cancer cell line (BGC-823), human female leukemic cell line (K562), and human normal lung epithelial cell line (BEAS-2B) were purchased from the Cell Resource Center (Peking Union Medical College Headquarters of National Infrastructure of Cell Line Resource, Beijing, China). The quality inspection report for cell lines is shown in supporting information (Figure S2). BGC-823, A549, and K562 cells were cultured in complete medium (RPMI-1640+10% FBS +1% P/S). MCF-7 and BEAS-2B cells were cultured in another complete medium (DMEM-H+10% FBS+1% P/S). MCF-7/ADR cells were cultured in complete medium with a low dose of ADR (RPMI-1640+10% FBS+1% P/S+800 ng/mL ADR). All the cells were cultured at 37 °C under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2.

The cellular uptake process was monitored on an inverted fluorescence microscope. The cells were seeded onto 12-well plates at the density of 105 cells per well in the culture medium. After 24 h of incubation, the culture medium of each well was replaced with fresh medium containing the Arg-dots and/or ADR. After incubating for 4 h, the cells were treated with DAF-FM DA (5.0 μM) for 30 min. Subsequently, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed two times with PBS buffer before fluorescence imaging.

To investigate the in vitro toxicity of the Arg-dots, the cell viabilities were measured in the presence of the Arg-dots. The in vitro cytotoxicity of the Arg-dots was determined by using an MTT reagent [40]. Briefly, the cells were seeded onto 96-well plates at the density of 104 cells per well in the culture medium and incubated for 24 h to allow cell attachment. The culture medium of each well was replaced with 100 μL fresh medium containing the Arg-dots with various concentrations, and the cells were cultured with the Arg-dots for 48 h. After incubation, MTT (5 mg/mL) PBS stock solution was added to each well of the plate (the volume of the MTT test solution in each well was 1/10 of the total volume) and incubated for another 4 h. The medium containing unbound MTT was then removed carefully. The obtained blue formazan crystals were dissolved in 120 μL DMSO per well, and the absorbance was measured to evaluate the cell viability at the wavelength of 570 nm by using a microplate reader. Percentage of the viability was normalized according to the control cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vivo Evaluation of the Curative Effect
ce:section.ce:para: The animal experimental procedures were in agreement with the guidelines of the Animal Ethics Committee of China Medical University (assigned approval number: No. 1100111911073128). MCF-7/ADR cell lines were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10% FBS and 800 ng/mL ADR. Next, 5- to 6-week-old female BALB/c nude mice (CAnN.Cg-Foxn1nu/Crl) were injected subcutaneously in the left flank region with 1 × 107 MCF-7/ADR cells. Anticancer efficacy in vivo was investigated after the tumor volume reached at least 50 mm3 (day 1). At that point, tumor-bearing mice were divided into four groups (n = 5 per group) after minimizing weight and tumor size differences. Next, 50 µL of PBS (as a control), ADR dispersed in 50 µL of PBS (10 mg/kg), Arg-dots dispersed in 50 µL of PBS (100 mg/kg), or Arg-dots and ADR dispersed in 50 µL of PBS (100 mg/kg Arg-dots and 10 mg/kg ADR) were injected seven times on predetermined days (day 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, 16, and 19). Subcutaneous injection around the tumor was used to preclude any pharmacokinetic complications. Tumor dimensions in each group were measured on predetermined days by using a Vernier caliper. Tumor volume was calculated according to the following formula: volume = 0.5 × (tumor length) × (tumor width)2. Body weights were also monitored on predetermined days. The mice were sacrificed on day 22 after the first treatment. The tumors were collected, and their weights in each group were measured. The work described herein was performed in accordance with the National Institutes of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals.

Healthy female C57/BL mice (10 weeks old, n = 6 per group) were provided with standard food and water ad libitum, with a light cycle of 12-h on and 12-h off per day. Mice in the experimental group were injected with the PBS-dispersed Arg-dots for 4 weeks at the dose of 2.5 μg/g/d, followed by the dose of 250 μg/g/d for 1 week. Mice in the control group were injected with PBS for 5 weeks. Both groups were administered intravenously through the tail vein with a total volume of 100 μL each time. The mice were euthanized by cervical dislocation. The organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, were harvested and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 24 h. The fixed samples were embedded in paraffin and cut into 5-μm-thick sections. The tissue sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The images were obtained with an Axio Scope A1 microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

Statistical analyses were conducted using SPSS 20.0 (Chicago, IL, USA) and GraphPad Prism 9.1 software. Student's t-test (two-tailed) was used to compare the mean value of each column with the mean value of a control column. The significance of differences in specimen data was determined by a chi-square test. The Kaplan-Meier method was used to generate survival curves. *p<0.05, ** p<0.01, *** p<0.001, and **** p<0.0001 were considered to be statistically significant.

The Arg-dots exhibited a spherical shape with an average diameter ranging from 1.5 to 3.5 nm (Fig. 1 A). Particle size distribution analysis (random sampling of 15 particles from different regions of the TEM sample) demonstrated that the Arg-dots had a narrow size distribution (inset in Fig. 1A). The hydrodynamic diameter of the Arg-dots was 3.0 nm (Figure S3), which was consistent with that obtained from the TEM test. The Arg-dots were also well dispersed in PBS solution with a diameter of 3.2 nm and without any aggregation. The highly crystalline carbon structure is observed in the HRTEM image. The lattice fringe distance was ca. 0.16 nm. The Arg-dots exhibited typical characteristics of CDs, including small size, spherical morphology, and crystalline structure [41]. The guanidinium groups on the Arg-dots were confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 1B) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS spectra are shown in Figure S4 and Fig. 1C). As shown in the FT-IR spectrum of the Arg-dots, the peaks at 1640, 1024, and 794 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=N, C-N, and C-NH-C groups belonging to the guanidinium groups. The vibration band of C=C at 1640 cm−1 confirmed the carbonization of L-Arg under microwave irradiation. The carboxyl stretching vibration peak at 1585 cm−1 and its antisymmetric stretching peak at 1356 cm−1 had almost disappeared compared to that for L-Arg, which confirmed the successful purification of the Arg-dots. In the high-resolution N1s spectrum of the Arg-dots (Fig. 1C), the N1s peak showed three components due to the presence of the guanidinium groups at the surface of the Arg-dots, which could be attributed to N-H, C=N, and C-N bonds. The Sakaguchi reaction has been extensively used for the colorimetric determination of the guanidinium groups through the reaction between the guanidinium groups and hypohalites in the presence of α-phenols [42]. The Arg-dot solution turned red with Sakaguchi reagent, and the colored product showed an intense absorption peak at 500 nm (Fig. 1D). These results further verified the presence of the guanidinium groups on the Arg-dots. A standard curve was drawn by using L-Arg as the control sample based on chromogenic Sakaguchi reaction (Figure S5). The amount of the guanidinium groups in the Arg-dots was calibrated. The result revealed that 1 g Arg-dots contained an equal amount of the guanidinium groups compared to 0.8 g L-Arg. Hypothetically, all the raw materials (5.74 mmol arginine and 2.88 mmol ethylenediamine) were involved in the formation of the Arg-dots, and 1 g Arg-dots should contain approximately 0.85 g L-Arg and 0.15 g ethylenediamine. The difference between the experimental and theoretical values revealed the loss of the guanidinium groups in the formation of the Arg-dots.

The Arg-dots show an excitation wavelength (λex )-dependent PL behavior similar to most CDs (Fig. 2 A). The position of the fluorescence emission peaks of the Arg-dots showed a significant red-shift with the increase in λex . The emission peak intensity first increased and then decreased, and the most substantial emission peak appeared at 440 nm under the irradiation of 370 nm. The fluorescence QY of the Arg-dots was 6.88%, and their fluorescence decayed multiexponentially with two average lifetimes (τ1 = 1.5 ns and τ2 = 12.1 ns, fluorescence decay curve shown in Figure S6). This fluorescence property allows the Arg-dots to be used as a suitable fluorescent probe for fluorescence imaging of tumor cells. To investigate the intracellular localization of the Arg-dots, A549 tumor cells were treated with the Arg-dots. DAF-FM DA, a commercial NO-sensitive fluorescent probe, was also used together with the Arg-dots to monitor the release of NO in tumor cells. DAF-FM DA can trap NO to yield a highly green fluorescent triazole compound in cells, which allows direct identification of intracellular production and localization of NO [43]. The intracellular localization of the Arg-dots and DAF-FM DA was visualized by inverted fluorescent microscopy under different FL channels. The Arg-dots were readily visualized by their blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 385 nm (Fig. 2B), indicating that the Arg-dots had entered the tumor cells and were dispersed in the cytoplasm. The green fluorescence enabled to visualize DAF-FM DA under the irradiation of 470 nm (Fig. 2C), whereas the Arg-dots displayed extremely weak fluorescence emission. The merged image shows a high colocalization of the Arg-dots with NO (Fig. 2D), demonstrating that NO is endogenously generated in situ from the Arg-dots in the tumor cells. The intracellular localization of the Arg-dots and the generation of NO also laid the foundation for the subsequent antitumor cell experiments.

The extracellular NO release profiles were investigated by a Griess assay [44]. Compared to normal cells, cancer cells have a rich H2O2 environment due to an uncontrollable glycolytic metabolism [45]. The concentrations of H2O2 were set as the variables and regulated to monitor the NO release profiles of the Arg-dots. Fig. 3 A shows the NO release profiles of the Arg-dots incubated in PBS with various concentrations of H2O2. With an increase in H2O2 concentration from 0 to 25.0 mM, both NO release amount and the release rate of the Arg-dots increased markedly. The total NO release amount ([NO]T) of the Arg-dots was 20, 39, and 43 μM in the presence of 6.5, 12.5, and 25.0 mM H2O2, thus reaching high NO concentrations that can induce tumor apoptosis. Because of the detection limit of the current assay method, the [NO]T in the actual tumor environment with a relatively low concentration of H2O2 (ca. 5 μM–1 mM) [46] could not be assessed, and the following intracellular experiments were performed to compensate for the deficiencies of the assay method. The NO release half-life period of the Arg-dots (t1/2 ) decreased with the increase in H2O2 concentration due to an increased release rate ([H2O2]=6.5 mM, t1/2 =48 h; [H2O2]=12.5 mM, t1/2 =45 h; [H2O2]=25.0 mM, t1/2 =25 h); this indicated that the Arg-dots possess longer retention time in the blood with a low concentration of H2O2. The Arg-dots released approximately two-fold more NO than the equivalent amount of L-Arg, suggesting a considerably increased activity of L-Arg by the CD structure (Fig. 3B). After the release of NO, the size of the Arg-dots barely changed (Figure S7). However, at this time point, the characteristic peaks of the guanidinium groups (C-N and C-NH-C) in the FTIR spectrum of the Arg-dots had almost disappeared (Figure S8), indicating that the guanidinium groups were already consumed by H2O2. The XPS analysis also confirmed the loss of the guanidinium groups in the reaction through the decrease in the content of total nitrogen and amino nitrogen (Figure S9 and Table S1).

DFT calculations revealed that the introduction of CD structure optimized the electronic properties of the guanidinium groups. The Arg-dots were simulated using L-Arg-modified nitrogen-doped graphite structure (Figure S1). The calculated minimum distance between L-Arg and the graphite surface was 1.566 Å. The carbon atom of the graphite linked to L-Arg was pulled out of the graphite plane with a displacement of ca. 3 × 10−2 Å after covalent binding of L-Arg (Fig. 3C). This confirmed the existence of a weak interaction between L-Arg and graphite [47–49]. The positive charge in the guanidinium groups was delocalized due to the conjugation between the double bond and the lone nitrogen pair (Fig. 3C). The distance between nitrogen atoms and the ortho-carbon atom (C...N) in the guanidinium groups increased by 1 × 10−3 Å, resulting in a less stable covalent bond (C-N) and a more active site. The charge density difference was also determined by subtracting the charge density of L-Arg and graphite from the total electron density of the Arg-dots (Fig. 3D). The corresponding charge accumulation and depletion are shown in yellow and blue, respectively. The charge redistribution mainly occurred around the L-Arg and graphite interface. The electrons transferred from L-Arg to graphite through the interface because of the abovementioned weak interaction. The nitrogen atoms of the guanidinium groups attained more charge and higher activity. Bader charge analysis showed that ca. 0.102 electrons were transferred from L-Arg to the graphite, and the nitrogen atom of the guanidinium groups had 0.013 more electrons. To further investigate the nature of the interaction between L-Arg and graphite, the density of states (DOS) of L-Arg, graphite, and Arg-dots was calculated (Fig. 3E). The Arg-dots themselves are conducting materials and are beneficial for electron transfer.

The overlap of the partial density of states (PDOS) of L-Arg, graphite, and Arg-dots at -1.30 eV, -2.08 eV, and -2.59 eV relative to Ef, respectively, indicated weak hybridization between L-Arg and graphite in the Arg-dots [50]. The DOS of the nitrogen atom of the guanidinium groups of L-Arg and the Arg-dots is shown in Fig. 3F and Figure S10. Compared to L-Arg, the peak position of the Arg-dots was far from the Fermi level, which further confirmed the charge transfer at the boundary of L-Arg and graphite. MacFarlane's group has also confirmed that nitrogen-doped CDs have a high affinity to H2O2 and favor a direct charge transfer between the CDs and H2O2 [51]. This also facilitates the contact and reaction between the Arg-dots and H2O2.

Four tumor cell lines, including MCF-7, BGC-823, A549, and K562, and one normal cell line, BEAS-2B, were chosen for incubation with various concentrations of the Arg-dots to evaluate their antitumor activity. These tumor cells have been reported to contain a high level of H2O2 [52,53]. The cell viability was determined by an MTT assay. As indicated in Fig. 4 A, the cell viability of the normal BEAS-2B cells incubated with the Arg-dots remained above 94%, thus demonstrating the low toxicity of the Arg-dots to normal cells. In contrast, the survival of all four types of tumor cells declined with the increase in the concentration of the Arg-dots. In particular for A549 tumor cells, more than 80% of tumor cells were killed by the Arg-dots. The L-Arg control group showed the highest cellular lethality of ca. 20% (Figure S11A). These results demonstrate that the Arg-dots are ideal antitumor agents as they are safe for the normal cells but toxic for the tumor cells. The differential effects of the Arg-dots on normal and tumor cells can be speculated by relating to the intrinsic microenvironment of these cells, especially the endogenous H2O2 concentrations in these cells. Cancer cells produce a high amount of endogenous H2O2, which induces the Arg-dots to provide a high level of NO and promote cell apoptosis; in contrast, the H2O2 concentration in normal cells is too low to generate lethal or carcinogenic dose of NO. NO synthesis in a physiological environment is extremely complex and involves a series of biochemical compounds and reactions. It is difficult to reproduce the cellular environment in an extracellular experiment. Hence, the intracellular NO level was assessed in A549 tumor cells in the presence of various concentrations of the Arg-dots and L-Arg (Figure S12). The Arg-dots could release more NO than L-Arg within cells. With the increase in the concentration, the accumulative NO release of the Arg-dots at 48 h increased and reached the highest concentration of 6 μM (10 mg/mL). Comparatively, the accumulative NO release of L-Arg increased only slightly. Furthermore, the intracellular NO was stained by DAF-FM DA and analyzed by flow cytometry (Figure S13). With the increase in Arg-dot concentration, the NO level in A549 cells was markedly elevated. The results showed a good agreement with the observed antitumor activity of the Arg-dots, which indicates that the large amount of NO generated from the Arg-dots contributes to the enhanced anticancer effect.

MDR is a primary challenge in tumor therapy, which increases the efflux of anticancer drugs and decreases the effectiveness of chemotherapy. It has been suggested that MDR of tumor cells can be reversed after exposure to a NO chemosensitizer. To gain more insights into the curative effect of the Arg-dots against MDR, the MDR cell line MCF-7/ADR was chosen for incubation with the Arg-dots (Fig. 4B). Free ADR shows weak cytotoxicity against MCF-7/ADR cells. L-Arg showed weak cytotoxicity to MCF-7/ADR cells (Figure S11B). P-glycoprotein, the MDR1 protein (MDR1: multidrug resistance gene 1), is the major cause of MDR in tumors [54]. The downregulation of P-glycoprotein can confirm the reversal of MDR. Herein, P-glycoprotein was marked with a rabbit P-glycoprotein primary antibody and a fluorescent goat polyclonal anti-rabbit IgG H&L secondary antibody conjugated with Alexa Fluor 594. In the presence of the Arg-dots, the fluorescence intensity of P-glycoprotein decreased (Figure S14), revealing that P-glycoprotein expression was inhibited by the Arg-dots. Thus, the Arg-dots can not only provide a high level of NO for pro-NO cancer therapies, but they can also reverse MDR and improve the chemotherapeutic efficacy of ADR.

After confirming the enhanced anticancer activity of the Arg-dot/ADR in vitro, the evaluation of the Arg-dot/ADR in pro-NO cancer therapy was further performed on MCF-7/ADR tumor-bearing mice (n = 5/group). After intratumoral injection, the Arg-dot/ADR showed significantly enhanced antitumor efficacy in vivo (Fig. 4C and D). The tumor volume of the control mice with no treatment rapidly increased in 21 d, while the tumor growth of the treated mice was suppressed through the effect of ADR, Arg-dots, or Arg-dot/ADR. Notably, the Arg-dot/ADR group demonstrated better antitumor efficacy than the relative group (ADR or Arg-dots alone). This finding is in good agreement with the results of in vitro experiments and further confirms the MDR reversal effect of the Arg-dots in vivo. It should also be noted that the high antitumor potency and efficacy of the Arg-dot/ADR is not accompanied by a loss in body weight, suggesting that the Arg-dot/ADR is a safe therapy system (Fig. 4E and F).

The toxicity of the Arg-dots was evaluated on healthy female C57/BL mice (10 weeks old, n = 6/group) in vivo through quantitative and periodic injection. H&E staining images of the major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, were collected from the Arg-dot-treated mice and PBS-treated mice and exhibited few discrepancies (Fig. 5 A). The Arg-dots showed no apparent suppressive effects on body weight in both high-dose acute toxicity and low-dose chronic toxicity experiments (Fig. 5B and C). These results reveal the Arg-dots are almost nontoxic. The Arg-dots with an ultra-small size can be removed through kidney filtration and have a renal clearance property. The ultra-small size of the Arg-dots is also suitable for improving their penetration efficiency in deep tumors.

However, the ultra-small size of the Arg-dots is like a double-edged sword; it could lead to enrichment of the Arg-dots in tumor cells and long-term circulation of the nanomedicine, resulting in a high dose of the Arg-dots in pro-NO cancer therapy. This drawback can be addressed by adopting programmed smart size-switching nanocarriers in the subsequent work [55,56]. The nanocarriers can also achieve the simultaneous delivery of the Arg-dots and ADR to tumor tissues in a single nanosystem and thus enhance their combined effect in antitumor therapy.

This study presents a type of CDs with the guanidinium groups to produce high concentrations of NO in pro-NO cancer therapy. On the one hand, the Arg-dots can release two-fold more NO than an equivalent amount of L-Arg in the H2O2-rich tumor microenvironment without any additional external interventions. On the other hand, the Arg-dots can reverse the MDR of tumor cells and synergize with traditional chemotherapeutic drugs to generate a combined effect in antitumor therapy. The Arg-dots have many advantages over other NO donors, including simple method of preparation, low cost, high biocompatibility, and stable fluorescent properties, which provide a potentially multifunctional nanoplatform for future applications of the Arg-dots in cancer therapy.
The authors declare that they have no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873085) and the LiaoNing Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2007056).
